Reactive Apps

... without frameworks

Emiliano Pisu

Father of 2 lovely "monsters"

D&D master, player ... whatever

Senior Design Engineer, Sensei & Co-Host
@ Dev Dojo IT

linkedin.com/in/pixu1980
github.com/pixu1980
codepen.io/pixu1980
linkedin.com/company/dev-dojo-it/
youtube.com/@devdojo_it
twitch.tv/devdojo_it
@devdojo_it

Are you nuts?
without frameworks?

What about frameworks?

Frameworks are great, but they are not the only way to build reactive apps

What about the platform?

The web platform is evolving to support reactive apps without frameworks

What about the demo?

A Todo app with persistence, filters, bulk actions and a live event log

What about the code?

Signals, Proxy Store, DOM Parts and Tagged Templates

How this can be possible?

A central store, a tiny Signals graph, persistent DOM parts and tagged templates are enough to build a real app-shaped UI without shipping a framework runtime.

What are we going to
build and dissect today?

Topics

We'll cover the following topics:

  • Runtime: Signals, effects, model and repeat
  • Store: Proxy-based global state management
  • Signals: fine-grained reactivity for any value
  • Rendering: DOM-first approach with persistent parts
  • Directives: control flow and custom behavior with tagged templates
  • Demo: building a Todo app from scratch

The runtime core

  • Signals for local dependency graphs, computed values and effect scheduling
  • Proxy Store for path-aware mutations and a stable store:change contract
  • DOM Parts for persistent update slots instead of full tree replacement
  • Tagged Templates for declarative views without JSX or a compiler step

The rendering pipeline

  • model as a tiny two-way binding directive with explicit get/set
  • repeat as keyed range reuse for long lists and stable DOM nodes
  • Generator pipelines for lazy filtering before final materialization
  • Template caching via the stable identity of template literal strings

From primitives to a real demo

  • A Todo app with draft state, filters, category management and bulk actions
  • A live store:change panel wired to the same runtime as the app itself
  • Persistence through localStorage, not through hidden framework state
  • A few subtle bugs that reveal where the runtime still needs engineering discipline
Into the runtime
Into the runtime Into the runtime Into the runtime Into the runtime Into the runtime

The pitch

  • Build a reactive UI stack from platform primitives, not from framework abstractions
  • Use signals and DOM parts to target precise DOM updates instead of diffing the whole tree
  • Keep view authoring expressive with tagged templates, directives and keyed repeat
  • Ship a Todo demo with persistence, filters, bulk actions and a live event log

Why now?

  • Signals are converging on a shared vocabulary for state, computed values and effects
  • DOM Parts show how native DOM ranges can behave like stable update slots
  • Tagged templates already give us static structure plus dynamic insertion points
  • Proxy + CustomEvent make state inspection and tooling straightforward

The browser is catching up

The platform is getting closer to the primitives UI frameworks have been emulating for years. The point is not "frameworks bad", it is understanding how much of the stack can move back into the browser.

Not a final standard, but a very interesting direction

  • TC39 Signals are still Stage 1, so this talk uses a home-grown runtime, not a standards API
  • DOM Parts are still a proposal, so we emulate them with comment anchors and ranges
  • Key takeaway: the platform direction is coherent enough to prototype against it today

Scope and honesty

  • This is not a framework replacement manifesto
  • This is a deliberately small runtime to study data flow, rendering and tradeoffs
  • Some parts are fine-grained, some parts are intentionally coarse and explicit
  • We will look at the actual code, the actual demo and the actual bugs

Layered by responsibility, not by hype

The project is intentionally split into small layers: a Proxy-based store, a tiny signals engine, a template runtime based on DOM parts, and an app layer that composes them into a Todo demo.

Repository map

  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/core/store/* Proxy tree, snapshot cloning, store:change emission
  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/core/signals/* State, Computed, dependency tracking, microtask effects
  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/core/template-engine/* template compilation, parts, directives, keyed repeat
  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/state/* store setup, computed selectors, actions, persistence bridge
  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/views/app/* app shell subcomponents, panels and debug surface
  • reactive-apps-without-frameworks-demo/src/scripts/data/* seed state, generator filters, sorting pipeline

Data flow

  • A user action calls a store mutation or an action function
  • The store emits a store:change event with path, oldValue, newValue
  • The state bridge persists the snapshot, appends a debug entry, then bumps mainState
  • Signals and template parts update the minimum DOM needed for the new view state

End-to-end write path

  • Write: store.set('filters.search', value) or an action replacing a todo object
  • Observe: the Proxy emits one normalized event payload
  • Bridge: the event listener updates persistence, debug log and invalidation state
  • Render: the root effect re-runs the view, but parts reuse existing DOM nodes

Fine-grained parts

  • Signals only notify their own subscribers
  • Computed values lazily rebuild dependencies
  • Parts update ranges, attributes, properties or listeners in place

Deliberately coarse parts

  • The store does not track field-level reactive dependencies
  • mainState is a blunt invalidation bridge from store writes to the UI graph
  • This is acceptable here because DOM reuse still keeps actual updates local

A Proxy store with a stable event contract

  • The raw state is deep-cloned once at construction time
  • Nested objects are lazily wrapped in cached proxies via WeakMap
  • Each write emits one CustomEvent('store:change')
  • The emitted payload is serializable and detached from proxy internals

Platform APIs in play

  • Proxy intercepts get, set and deleteProperty
  • Reflect preserves native object semantics
  • WeakMap keeps proxy identity stable across repeated reads
  • CustomEvent transports mutation metadata through the browser event system
  • localStorage stores the snapshot, not framework-owned hidden state
 

createProxy(target, path) {
  if (this.proxyCache.has(target))
    return this.proxyCache.get(target)

  const proxy = new Proxy(target, {
    get: (raw, key, receiver) => {
      const value = Reflect.get(raw, key, receiver)
      return isObject(value)
        ? this.createProxy(value, [...path, key])
        : value
    },
    set: (raw, key, value, receiver) => {
      const nextPath = [...path, key]
      const oldValue = raw[key]
      const prepared = clonePlainValue(value)
      const result = Reflect.set(raw, key, prepared, receiver)
      if (oldValue !== prepared)
        this.emitChange(nextPath, oldValue, prepared)
      return result
    }
  })

Why the demo and the log stay in sync

  • The debug panel is driven by the same store:change events as the rest of the runtime
  • Internal writes to debug.logs are protected by a re-entrancy guard
  • Persistence now happens after the optional debug entry is appended, so the saved snapshot matches the visible state
  • Paused logging suppresses new debug entries; unpausing emits its own debug.paused mutation
 

window.addEventListener('store:change', (event) => {
  if (isWritingDebugLog) return

  if (!store.state.debug.paused &&
      event.detail.path !== 'debug.logs') {
    isWritingDebugLog = true
    try {
      store.state.debug.logs = [
        {
          id: crypto.randomUUID(),
          timestamp: new Date().toLocaleTimeString('en-GB'),
          ...event.detail,
        },
        ...store.state.debug.logs,
      ].slice(0, 30)
    } finally {
      isWritingDebugLog = false
    }
  }

  localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY,
    JSON.stringify(store.snapshot()))
  mainState.set(performance.now())
})

Field-level mutations

  • draft.title and filters.search log exactly one field path per keystroke
  • They are produced by store.set(path, value) through the model directive

Todo item mutations

  • updateTodo() replaces one array item with a patched object
  • The log therefore shows paths like todos.1, not todos.1.title
  • That is a conscious simplification: predictable patches over deep structural tracking

Signals: a tiny dependency graph

  • Signal.State owns mutable state plus an equality strategy
  • Signal.Computed tracks dependencies by observing who calls .get()
  • effect() is just a collector whose callback is re-scheduled when dependencies invalidate
  • There is no scheduler framework here, only a microtask queue and sets of subscribers

How dependency collection works

  • collectorStack keeps track of the currently executing computed or effect
  • Any signal read during that execution registers itself as a dependency
  • Computed instances unsubscribe from stale dependencies before recomputing
  • The graph is rebuilt from reads, not declared up front
 

track() {
  const current =
    collectorStack[collectorStack.length - 1]
  if (current) current.addDependency(this)
}

evaluate() {
  if (!this.dirty) return this.cached
  this.cleanupDependencies()
  collectorStack.push(this)
  try {
    this.cached = this.compute()
    this.dirty = false
    return this.cached
  } finally {
    collectorStack.pop()
  }
}

Why microtasks matter

  • Multiple signal invalidations in the same tick should not trigger N renders
  • schedule() deduplicates effect collectors in a Set
  • queueMicrotask() flushes them once the current stack is done
  • This is enough to batch render work without a heavyweight scheduler
 

function schedule(effect) {
  scheduled.add(effect)
  if (flushing) return
  flushing = true

  queueMicrotask(() => {
    try {
      while (scheduled.size > 0) {
        const batch = [...scheduled]
        scheduled.clear()
        for (const job of batch) job.run()
      }
    } finally {
      flushing = false
    }
  })
}

mainState is the bridge, not the final dream

  • The store is event-based, not signal-native
  • The bridge signal uses equals: () => false so every store write invalidates dependents
  • visibleTodos, summary, debugLogs and the root render effect all read it
  • This is a coarse invalidation strategy, but it keeps the integration explicit and easy to reason about

Tagged templates as a view IR

  • html(strings, ...values) does not render immediately
  • It returns a template-result record with static shape plus dynamic values
  • The static strings array is a stable cache key for template compilation
  • This gives us JSX-like ergonomics without a compiler or a virtual DOM

Compilation step

  • Insert placeholder markers into the HTML string
  • Parse once with <template> into an inert DocumentFragment
  • Walk the fragment to discover child, attribute, property and event descriptors
  • Clone the fragment for each concrete instance and resolve parts by node path
 

for (let index = 0; index < strings.length - 1; index += 1) {
  const chunk = strings[index]
  markup += chunk
  const attributeMatch =
    chunk.match(/([.@]?[-\\w:]+)\\s*=\\s*(?:\"|'|)?$/)

  markup += attributeMatch
    ? `__part_${index}__`
    : ``
}

const template = document.createElement('template')
template.innerHTML = markup
const walker = document.createTreeWalker(
  template.content,
  NodeFilter.SHOW_ELEMENT | NodeFilter.SHOW_COMMENT
)

DOM Parts are persistent update slots

  • ChildNodePart owns a start and end comment and updates the range between them
  • AttributePart writes plain HTML attributes and hosts the model directive
  • PropertyPart writes native JS properties such as .value or .disabled
  • EventPart attaches stable DOM listeners

How child ranges work

  • Each dynamic child node is replaced by comment anchors
  • Clearing a part means removing nodes between start and end
  • Reusing a template means updating existing parts, not reparsing HTML
  • Range movement powers keyed repeat without reconstructing every child
 

commitTemplate(result) {
  if (this.currentTemplateInstance?.strings === result.strings) {
    this.currentTemplateInstance.update(result.values)
    return
  }

  clearRange(this.start, this.end)
  const instance = new TemplateInstance(result.strings)
  this.currentTemplateInstance = instance
  instance.update(result.values)
  this.start.parentNode.insertBefore(instance.fragment, this.end)
}

Why there is no VDOM here

  • The static tree is compiled once and cached by strings
  • Instances clone DOM fragments directly, not virtual nodes
  • Updates are dispatched to concrete parts with concrete DOM references
  • The runtime never needs a generic "diff every child" pass
 

export function render(result, container) {
  let rootPart = container.__rootPart
  if (!rootPart) {
    const start = document.createComment('root:start')
    const end = document.createComment('root:end')
    container.textContent = ''
    container.append(start, end)
    rootPart = new ChildNodePart(start, end)
    container.__rootPart = rootPart
  }
  rootPart.setValue(result)
}

What gets updated on every store write?

  • The root effect re-runs appView()
  • Template instances with the same shape are reused
  • Only the parts that see changed values commit DOM updates
  • So the render function is coarse, but DOM mutation is still localized

model and repeat carry most of the DX

  • model turns a DOM field into a tiny explicit binding contract
  • repeat gives the runtime keyed list reuse with stable ranges
  • Neither directive is magic: both are plain data handled by parts
  • This is where the demo starts feeling "framework-like" without actually depending on one

The model contract

  • get() reads from the store or from a specific todo item
  • set(value) writes back through store.set() or an action
  • signal decides when the DOM control should resync
  • event and prop let the binding target text inputs, checkboxes and selects
 

export function todoModel(todoId, field, options = {}) {
  return model({
    signal: mainState,
    get: () => getTodoById(todoId)?.[field]
      ?? (options.prop === 'checked' ? false : ''),
    set: (value) => updateTodo(todoId, { [field]: value }),
    ...options,
  })
}

The repeat algorithm

  • Iterate the next list from the end so insertion can happen before a stable reference node
  • Reuse an existing block when the key is already known
  • Move the block range if its current DOM position is wrong
  • Create new blocks for unseen keys and remove blocks for vanished keys
 

for (let index = list.length - 1; index >= 0; index -= 1) {
  const item = list[index]
  const itemKey = key(item)
  let block = state.blocks.get(itemKey)

  if (!block) {
    block = createBlock(itemKey, referenceNode)
    block.part.setValue(renderItem(item))
  } else {
    if (!isRangeBeforeReference(block.start, block.end, referenceNode))
      moveRangeBefore(block.start, block.end, referenceNode)
    if (block.item !== item)
      block.part.setValue(renderItem(item))
  }

  block.item = item
  nextBlocks.set(itemKey, block)
  referenceNode = block.start
}

A subtle bug: <select> values can desync during initial render

  • In a template like <select model=\${...}>\${options}</select>, the binding part is visited before the child options part
  • That means the binding can set select.value before the matching <option> exists in the DOM
  • The fix is pragmatic: for HTMLSelectElement, sync immediately and then resync in a microtask after the render pass completes
  • This is exactly the kind of edge case a tiny runtime exposes very clearly
 

const syncAfterRender = () => {
  sync()
  if (this.element instanceof HTMLSelectElement) {
    queueMicrotask(() => {
      if (this._modelBinding === binding) sync()
    })
  }
}

if (config.signal && isSignalLike(config.signal)) {
  this.bindSignal(config.signal, syncAfterRender)
  return
}

syncAfterRender()
    

repeat(visibleTodos, (todo) => todo.id, (todo) => html`
  <article class=${todo.completed ? 'todo-card is-done' : 'todo-card'}>
    <input class="todo-title"
      model=${todoModel(todo.id, 'title')} />

    ${categorySelect(
      todoModel(todo.id, 'category', { event: 'change' })
    )}

    <select model=${todoModel(todo.id, 'priority', { event: 'change' })}>
      ${priorityOptions()}
    </select>
  </article>
`)

The demo is a real app slice, not a toy counter

  • A draft form for new todos
  • Category management and multiple filters
  • Bulk actions over the currently visible subset
  • A debug panel driven by the same mutation events used for persistence and invalidation

Persistent state

  • todos array with title, notes, category, priority, due date, completion and selection flags
  • categories list reused by draft form and todo cards
  • draft values for quick add
  • filters for search, category, status, priority and sorting

Ephemeral-but-visible state

  • debug.paused toggles the event panel
  • debug.logs stores the latest 30 mutation entries
  • mainState is the render invalidation bridge
  • summary, visibleTodos and debugLogs are computed selectors over the store

Computed graph

  • visibleTodos filters and sorts from raw store state
  • summary derives counts once and feeds smaller computed signals
  • visibleLabel turns machine state into UI copy
  • debugLogs exposes the current event panel payload
 

export const visibleTodos = new Signal.Computed(() => {
  mainState.get()
  return pipelineTodos(store.state.todos, store.state.filters)
})

export const summary = new Signal.Computed(() => {
  mainState.get()
  // derive total, completed, open, selected, visible
})

Generator-first filtering

  • Each filter is a generator that yields only matching items
  • Search, category, status and priority are composed before sorting
  • Materialization only happens at the sort step
  • That keeps the pipeline explicit and cheap to inspect
 

export function pipelineTodos(todos, filters) {
  const iterator = filterByPriority(
    filterByStatus(
      filterByCategory(
        filterBySearch(fromArray(todos), filters.search),
        filters.category
      ),
      filters.status
    ),
    filters.priority
  )
  return sortTodos(iterator, filters.sortBy, filters.sortDir)
}

Action strategy

  • addTodo() prepends a new object and resets the draft
  • updateTodo() patches by replacing one todo object
  • toggleAllSelected(), deleteSelected(), deleteCompleted() operate on the whole array
  • resetDemo() replaces the entire store with fresh seed data
 

export function updateTodo(id, patch) {
  const index = store.state.todos.findIndex(
    (todo) => todo.id === id
  )
  if (index < 0) return

  const current = store.state.todos[index]
  store.state.todos[index] = {
    ...current,
    ...patch,
  }
}

The runtime is not theoretical. It is running here.

The embedded app uses the same store, signals, directives and template engine discussed in the deck. Editing a field, filtering the list or toggling bulk actions mutates the same state graph and feeds the same event log.

What this demo actually proves

  • You can get a lot of practical UI behavior from platform primitives and a few disciplined conventions
  • A tiny runtime makes architecture and bugs legible
  • The browser already gives us enough pieces to experiment seriously
  • The hard part is no longer "can it work?", but "how far do we want to maintain it ourselves?"

Wrapping up

The DOM can move back to the center of the rendering story. Signals, parts and tagged templates are enough to build a reactive app, as long as we stay honest about the missing abstractions and the engineering cost.

Strengths

  • DOM-first rendering, with node reuse instead of virtual tree diffing
  • Small surface area, so the runtime stays readable and debuggable
  • Concrete DX, with signals, directives and expressive templates
  • Observable state, because every write has a path and an event payload

Tradeoffs

  • The standards story is still evolving, so some primitives are emulated
  • The runtime still needs real engineering, not just enthusiasm
  • It is easy to accidentally rebuild framework complexity in a worse form
  • Great as a learning base, not as a universal dogma

Technical lessons from the demo

  • A coarse invalidation bridge can still produce incremental DOM updates if parts stay stable
  • model on <select> needs post-render resync because options materialize after the binding
  • A live event log is only useful if it describes the final persisted state, not an intermediate one
  • Small runtimes make bugs easier to understand because the entire stack still fits in your head

References and status

This deck runs on Reveal.js 6.0.0. The talk borrows ideas from TC39 Signals and the DOM Parts proposal, but the code you saw is an explicit, local implementation built for learning and experimentation.

Vanilla JS is not about giving up DX. It is about understanding which abstractions are essential, which are optional, and how far the platform can already take us.
